questions.stem: After fertilization, the first cells produced are:
questions.explanation: Fertilization produces unspecialized cells that later develop into specialized cells by differentiation.

questions.scenario: In an early embryo, chemical gradients form.
questions.stem: What is the effect of these gradients?

options.text: They influence gene expression, guiding differentiation

questions.explanation: Gradients in the early embryo affect gene expression, helping direct which cells become specialized in which ways.

questions.stem: Which two properties define stem cells?

options.text: The ability to divide endlessly and to differentiate

questions.explanation: Stem cells can divide endlessly and can differentiate along different pathways.

questions.stem: Which is an example of a stem cell niche in adult humans?
questions.explanation: Bone marrow and hair follicles are examples of stem cell niches in adult humans.

questions.stem: A stem cell niche can either maintain its cells or:
questions.explanation: A stem cell niche can maintain the cells or promote their proliferation and differentiation.

questions.scenario: Cells in a very early animal embryo can form any cell type of the body.
questions.stem: These cells are:
questions.explanation: Cells in early-stage animal embryos are totipotent, able to form any cell type.

questions.scenario: Stem cells in adult bone marrow can form several, but not all, cell types.
questions.stem: These cells are:
questions.explanation: Adult tissue stem cells such as those in bone marrow are multipotent, forming a limited range of cell types.

questions.stem: Which is the correct order of decreasing potency?
questions.explanation: Early embryo cells are totipotent, soon becoming pluripotent, while adult stem cells are multipotent.

questions.stem: Cell size varies with specialization. Which of these is among the largest human cells?
questions.explanation: Cell size is an aspect of specialization; the egg cell is one of the largest human cells.

questions.scenario: As a cell gets larger, its volume grows faster than its surface area.
questions.stem: What does this mean for its surface area-to-volume ratio?
questions.explanation: Volume increases faster than surface area, so the surface area-to-volume ratio decreases as a cell enlarges.

questions.stem: Exchange of materials across a cell's surface depends on its surface area, while the need for exchange depends on its:
questions.explanation: Exchange depends on surface area, but the need for exchange depends on volume, constraining cell size.

questions.scenario: A cube model is used to show how surface area-to-volume ratio changes with size.
questions.stem: Why is a cube a useful model here?

options.text: Scale factors operate the same way as in real cells, though it is simpler
It removes the need for calculation

questions.explanation: Models are simplified versions of complex systems; a cube models surface-area-to-volume scaling despite being simpler than a cell.

questions.stem: Which best describes the differentiation ability of a stem cell?

options.text: It can develop along different pathways into specialized cells
It can only ever become one fixed cell type from the start
It loses its genome when dividing

questions.explanation: Stem cells can differentiate along different pathways, in addition to dividing endlessly.

questions.stem: Differentiation is the process by which cells:
questions.explanation: Unspecialized cells develop into specialized cells through the process of differentiation.

questions.stem: Which human cell is specialized to be very small and lacks a nucleus when mature?
questions.explanation: Cell size reflects specialization; mature red blood cells are small and, in humans, lack a nucleus.

questions.stem: Neurons can be very long cells. This length is an example of:

options.text: Specialization affecting cell size and shape

questions.explanation: The range of cell sizes, including long neurons, illustrates size as an aspect of specialization.

questions.scenario: A very large cell struggles to exchange enough materials with its environment.
questions.stem: Why?

options.text: Its surface area is too small relative to its volume
Its surface area is too large
It has no need for exchange

questions.explanation: As cells enlarge, the falling surface area-to-volume ratio limits exchange, constraining cell size.

questions.stem: Which is a second example of a stem cell niche in adult humans?
questions.explanation: Bone marrow and hair follicles are the suggested examples of adult stem cell niches.

questions.scenario: Cells in an early embryo soon lose totipotency but can still form most body cell types.
questions.stem: These cells are now:
questions.explanation: Early embryo cells are totipotent but soon become pluripotent.

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly describes early development?

options.text: Fertilization → unspecialized cells → differentiation → specialized cells

questions.explanation: Fertilization produces unspecialized cells, which differentiate into specialized cells.

questions.stem: Why are stem cells valuable in medicine and development?

options.text: They can both self-renew and differentiate into needed cell types

questions.explanation: Stem cells' ability to divide endlessly and differentiate makes them valuable for replacing specialized cells.

questions.scenario: A cube has sides of 2 units.
questions.stem: Compared with a cube of side 1 unit, its surface area-to-volume ratio is:
questions.explanation: Larger cubes have smaller surface area-to-volume ratios, modelling why large cells exchange materials less efficiently.

questions.stem: The wide range of cell sizes in humans, from tiny sperm to long muscle fibres, reflects:

options.text: That all cells are the same

questions.explanation: Differences in cell size are part of how cells are specialized for their particular functions.

questions.stem: The location where stem cells are maintained in the body is called a:
questions.explanation: A stem cell niche is the location that maintains stem cells or promotes their proliferation and differentiation.

questions.stem: A totipotent cell differs from a multipotent cell in that a totipotent cell can form:

options.text: A wider range of cell types

questions.explanation: Totipotent cells can form all cell types, whereas multipotent cells form only a limited range.

questions.stem: Gradients of signalling molecules in an embryo lead to differentiation by:

options.text: Switching different genes on or off in different cells

questions.explanation: Gradients influence gene expression, switching genes on or off, so cells differentiate differently.

questions.stem: Which mathematical relationship constrains how large a single cell can be?
questions.explanation: The surface area-to-volume ratio limits cell size because exchange depends on area while need depends on volume.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a property of stem cells?

options.text: Being fully specialized and unable to change

questions.explanation: Stem cells are defined by endless division and the ability to differentiate; they are not already fully specialized.

questions.stem: Comparing a sperm cell and an egg cell in humans shows that:

options.text: Gametes can differ greatly in size
All gametes are the same size

questions.explanation: Male and female gametes illustrate the range of cell size that comes with specialization.

questions.stem: Which statement about potency is correct?

options.text: Adult bone marrow stem cells are multipotent
Adult bone marrow stem cells are totipotent
All adult stem cells are pluripotent

questions.explanation: Adult tissue stem cells such as those in bone marrow are multipotent, unlike the totipotent/pluripotent embryo cells.

questions.stem: A model such as a cube is described as a simplified version of a complex system. This is because models:

options.text: Represent the key features while leaving out complexity
Are always more complex than reality

questions.explanation: Models simplify complex systems while still capturing key relationships, such as surface-area-to-volume scaling.

questions.stem: All specialized cells in one organism generally contain:
questions.explanation: Differentiation arises from differential gene expression, not from cells having different genes.

questions.stem: Which property allows a stem cell population to be maintained over time?
questions.explanation: The capacity to divide endlessly (self-renew) maintains the stem cell population.

questions.stem: Striated muscle fibres are notable for being:
questions.explanation: Striated muscle fibres are among the larger, elongated cells, illustrating size as specialization.

questions.scenario: A cell needs a high rate of exchange with its surroundings.
questions.stem: Which adaptation increases its surface area-to-volume ratio?
questions.explanation: Adaptations such as flattening, microvilli and invagination increase the surface area-to-volume ratio for exchange.

questions.stem: Proximal convoluted tubule cells in the nephron have many microvilli. This adaptation:

options.text: Removes the need for transport proteins

questions.explanation: Microvilli on proximal convoluted tubule cells increase surface area, aiding reabsorption in the nephron.

questions.stem: The flattened, biconcave shape of an erythrocyte is an adaptation that:

options.text: Increases its surface area-to-volume ratio for gas exchange

questions.explanation: Flattening of erythrocytes increases the surface area-to-volume ratio, improving gas exchange.

questions.stem: Type I pneumocytes in the alveoli are adapted for gas exchange by being:
questions.explanation: Type I pneumocytes are extremely thin to reduce the distance over which gases diffuse.

questions.stem: Type II pneumocytes are adapted to:

options.text: Secrete surfactant into the alveolar lumen

questions.explanation: Type II pneumocytes contain many lamellar bodies (secretory vesicles) that discharge surfactant into the alveolus.

questions.scenario: The alveolar epithelium contains both type I and type II pneumocytes.
questions.stem: This shows that a tissue may:

options.text: Contain more than one cell type for its overall function
Only ever contain one cell type
Be made of a single cell

questions.explanation: Alveolar epithelium shows that different adaptations (thin cells and surfactant-secreting cells) can combine in one tissue.

questions.stem: Both cardiac muscle cells and striated muscle fibres contain:
questions.explanation: Both cardiac muscle cells and striated muscle fibres contain contractile myofibrils.

questions.stem: A difference between cardiac muscle cells and striated (skeletal) muscle fibres is:

options.text: Cardiac cells are branched; skeletal fibres are unbranched

questions.explanation: Cardiac muscle cells are branched, while skeletal muscle fibres are unbranched; they also differ in length and nuclei number.

questions.scenario: A striated muscle fibre is very long and contains many nuclei.
questions.stem: This raises the question of:

options.text: Whether a striated muscle fibre should be regarded as a single cell
Whether it is a plant cell

questions.explanation: Because striated muscle fibres are large and multinucleate, there is discussion about whether a fibre is truly a single cell.

questions.stem: A human sperm cell is specialized for its function by having:

options.text: A flagellum for swimming and many mitochondria
A large food store and no movement

questions.explanation: Sperm are adapted with a flagellum for movement and mitochondria to power swimming.

questions.stem: A human egg cell is adapted to its function by:

options.text: Being large with food reserves for the early embryo
Having a flagellum for rapid swimming

questions.explanation: The egg cell is large and stores nutrients to support the early development of the embryo.

questions.stem: Which set of features all increase a cell's surface area-to-volume ratio?
questions.explanation: Flattening, microvilli and invagination are adaptations that raise the surface area-to-volume ratio.

questions.scenario: Surfactant reduces surface tension in the alveoli.
questions.stem: Which cell type produces it?
questions.explanation: Type II pneumocytes discharge surfactant from lamellar bodies into the alveolar lumen.

questions.stem: Which feature is used to compare cardiac and skeletal muscle?

options.text: Branching and the length and number of nuclei
The presence of a cell wall

questions.explanation: Cardiac and skeletal muscle are compared by branching and by the length and number of nuclei, among other features.

questions.stem: Adaptations that increase surface area are especially important in cells specialized for:
questions.explanation: Cells specialized for absorption or exchange, like tubule and alveolar cells, benefit from a larger surface area.

questions.stem: Comparing sperm and egg cells shows that gametes are:

options.text: Each specialized for their distinct roles in reproduction

questions.explanation: Sperm (motile, mitochondria-rich) and eggs (large, nutrient-rich) are each specialized for their reproductive roles.
